title: Training vs Inference
lesson:
Every AI model lives two very different lives. First it goes to school. Then it goes to work. School is called training: the slow, expensive process where the model learns from huge piles of text. Work is called inference: the fast, cheap process where the finished model actually answers your questions.

Confusing the two is one of the most common beginner mistakes, so let's pull them apart clearly.

The cooking analogy

Think of a chef. Training is the years she spends in culinary school: tasting, failing, adjusting, building up instincts. It's a one-time investment, it's grueling, and it happens before any customer walks in.

Inference is a Tuesday lunch service: a customer orders, she cooks the dish, it's gone in twenty minutes. She isn't learning during service. She's using what she already knows. An LLM is the same. By the time you type into a chatbot, the model has already finished school. It is not learning from your message.

What actually happens during training

During training, the model reads text and tries to predict the next token (a token is a chunk of text, often a word or part of a word). When it guesses wrong, a step called backpropagation (working backwards through the network to see which knobs caused the error) nudges its millions or billions of parameters (the internal numbers, or 'knobs', that hold everything the model knows).

Each nudge is tiny. The model sees trillions of tokens and makes the nudge trillions of times. This is why training a frontier model can cost millions of dollars and run for weeks on thousands of GPUs (specialised chips built for the heavy maths AI needs).

What actually happens during inference

Inference is a one-way street. Your prompt goes in, flows forward through the frozen parameters once, and out comes a prediction for the next token. Then it does it again for the next token, and the next. No knobs move. No backpropagation.

That's why inference is thousands of times cheaper per request than training, and why it can run on a laptop or phone while training cannot. Here is the whole difference in two lines of pseudo-code:

# TRAINING: measure error, then adjust the knobs
loss = compare(model(text), correct_next_token)
loss.backward()      # backpropagation: find the blame
optimizer.step()     # nudge every parameter a little

# INFERENCE: just run forward, change nothing
next_token = model(prompt)   # no .backward(), no .step()

That missing .backward() is the entire distinction. Training adjusts; inference only runs.

The library analogy

Picture a librarian who has memorised an enormous library. Training was the lifetime spent reading every book and organising the shelves in her head. Inference is you walking up and asking 'where's a good mystery novel?' She answers instantly from memory.

Crucially, your question does not add a new book to the library. Many people assume that chatting with ChatGPT 'teaches it' things. It doesn't. Within a single conversation it can remember what you said (that's the context window, its short-term working memory), but once the chat ends, that memory is gone and the model's permanent knowledge is unchanged.

Side by side

TrainingInference
Model learns; parameters changeModel uses; parameters frozen
Runs forward AND backwardRuns forward only
Done once (or occasionally), takes days–weeksHappens on every request, takes milliseconds–seconds
Hugely expensive, needs many GPUsCheap per call, can run on modest hardware
Needs labelled or raw text datasetsNeeds only the user's prompt

Where this matters in real products

This split shapes almost every decision you'll make. A few rules of thumb:

  • Want the model to know your company's private documents? You usually do not retrain it. Instead you feed those documents into the prompt at inference time using RAG (fetching relevant text and pasting it into the prompt).
  • Want to genuinely change the model's behaviour or style? Use fine-tuning (a short, cheaper round of extra training on your own examples), often via LoRA so you only nudge a small slice of the parameters.
  • Watch the bill: training is paid once, but you pay for inference every single time a user hits 'send'. At scale, inference is usually most of your cost.

💡 Rule of thumb: if you want the model to know more facts right now, reach for the prompt and RAG. If you want it to behave differently in general, reach for fine-tuning. Reach for full training almost never.
takeaways: Training is school (parameters change); inference is the day job (frozen).
Inference is forward-only — no .backward(), so chatting never teaches the model.
Add facts with prompts/RAG, change behaviour with fine-tuning, retrain almost never.

inference just runs</text><text class="dg-sub" x="185" y="68" text-anchor="middle">TRAINING (rare, expensive)</text><text class="dg-sub" x="535" y="68" text-anchor="middle">INFERENCE (every request)</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="110" y1="135" x2="110" y2="172" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect class="dg-box" x="40" y="95" width="140" height="40" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="110" y="120" text-anchor="middle">Training text</text><rect class="dg-box dg-box-accent" x="40" y="175" width="230" height="50" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="155" y="205" text-anchor="middle">Forward → guess next token</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="120" y1="225" x2="120" y2="247" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect class="dg-box dg-box-warn" x="40" y="250" width="230" height="50" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="155" y="273" text-anchor="middle">Backprop nudges parameters</text><text class="dg-sub" x="155" y="291" text-anchor="middle">knobs CHANGE</text><path class="dg-edge" d="M270,275 C320,275 320,200 272,200" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><text class="dg-sub" x="330" y="242" text-anchor="middle">repeat</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="450" y1="120" x2="498" y2="120" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect class="dg-box" x="410" y="100" width="40" height="40" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="430" y="125" text-anchor="middle">Q</text><rect class="dg-box dg-box-2" x="500" y="95" width="180" height="50" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="590" y="118" text-anchor="middle">Frozen parameters</text><text class="dg-sub" x="590" y="136" text-anchor="middle">knobs DON'T change</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="590" y1="145" x2="590" y2="183" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect class="dg-box dg-box-good" x="500" y="190" width="180" height="50" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="590" y="220" text-anchor="middle">Forward pass only → answer</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Training loops forward and backward to change the model's knobs; inference runs forward once and leaves them frozen.

quiz.q: You tell a chatbot 'My cat is named Pixel,' it replies correctly, but a brand-new chat doesn't know the name. Why?
options: The fact lived only in the context window during inference; parameters never changed.
The model trained on your message but forgot it overnight.
Backpropagation ran but the optimizer step was skipped.
The new chat used a different, older version of the model.
quiz.explain: Inference is forward-only, so the parameters stay frozen. 'Pixel' existed only in that conversation's context window, which a fresh chat does not share.

quiz.q: Which single line of code is the real dividing line between training and inference?
quiz.explain: Inference also does a forward pass, so that isn't the distinction. Only training runs .backward() to compute how to nudge the parameters.

quiz.q: A startup wants its support bot to answer from 5,000 private help-desk articles that change weekly. Best first approach?
options: Fully pretrain a new model on the articles.
Fine-tune the model every week on the articles.
Use RAG to fetch relevant articles into the prompt at inference time.
Paste all 5,000 articles into every system prompt.
quiz.explain: Facts that change often are best injected at inference via RAG. Retraining or fine-tuning weekly is slow and costly, and dumping everything into the prompt blows the context window and budget.

quiz.q: For a chatbot with millions of daily users, which cost usually dominates over the product's lifetime?
options: Inference, because you pay per request forever.
Training, because it costs millions up front.
Storage of the model weights on disk.
Tokenization, since every message must be tokenized.
quiz.explain: Training is a one-time cost, but inference is paid on every single request. At scale those per-call costs add up to far more than the initial training.

mentalModel: Training is school: slow, expensive, done once, and it rewires the brain. Inference is the day job: fast, cheap, done constantly, and the brain stays exactly as it was. Chatting with a model is hiring it for an hour, not sending it back to school.
mistakes: Thinking your chat 'teaches' the model. During inference the parameters are frozen. Your message lives only in the context window and vanishes when the chat ends; the model's permanent knowledge never changes.
Confusing the context window with learning. A model 'remembering' earlier messages in a conversation is short-term memory inside one request, not training. Close the tab and it's forgotten.

Reaching for fine-tuning to add facts. If you just need the model to know your docs, use RAG at inference time. Fine-tuning changes behaviour and style, and is a poor, expensive way to inject fresh facts.
Budgeting for training but forgetting inference. Training is a one-time cost; inference is paid on every request forever. At scale, inference usually dominates the bill.
Assuming inference needs the same giant hardware as training. Inference runs forward only, so a quantized model can run on a laptop or phone, even though training it needed a GPU cluster.
Believing a model 'updates itself' over time. A deployed model is static until a human deliberately retrains or fine-tunes it and ships a new version. It does not drift or improve on its own.
exercise:
Grab any chatbot and run a small experiment to feel the training/inference line.

  1. Tell it a made-up fact about yourself, e.g. 'My pet dragon is named Kevin.'
  2. In the same conversation, ask 'What is my dragon's name?' It should answer Kevin (context window at work).
  3. Open a brand-new chat and ask the same question. It won't know. Write one sentence explaining why, using the words 'inference' and 'parameters'.

Then list three things you'd want a model to know for a real app of your own, and decide for each: prompt/RAG, fine-tuning, or full training?

Stretch goal: Estimate rough monthly cost for a chatbot with 10,000 users sending 20 messages each. Look up a real API's price per million tokens, assume roughly 1,000 tokens per message, and explain why this is an inference cost, not a training cost.
solution:
New-chat answer: the model can't recall Kevin because nothing was learned. During inference the parameters stayed frozen, and the only place 'Kevin' existed was the previous chat's context window, which a fresh conversation doesn't share.

Routing the three facts: a private knowledge base → RAG; a consistent tone or output format → fine-tuning (often LoRA); a brand-new base capability → full training (almost never worth it for a single app).

Stretch: 10,000 × 20 × 1,000 ≈ 200 million tokens/month. At, say, roughly $0.50 per million input tokens that's around $100/month, before counting the model's replies. It's an inference cost because you pay it on every message; the model's training was already paid for once by whoever built it.
summary: Training is the rare, expensive 'school' phase where the model reads mountains of text and its parameters change via backpropagation. Inference is the everyday 'work' phase: a single forward pass that produces an answer while the parameters stay frozen. To add knowledge, prefer prompts and RAG; to change behaviour, fine-tune; full training almost never. And remember your ongoing bill is mostly inference.

keyTerms.def: The learning phase where a model reads huge amounts of text and adjusts its internal numbers (parameters) to predict text better. Slow, expensive, done occasionally.
The using phase where a finished model takes your prompt and produces an answer. Fast, cheap per call, and the parameters never change.
The millions or billions of internal numbers, like knobs, that store everything the model has learned. Training tunes them; inference just reads them.
The 'work backwards to assign blame' step in training that figures out how to nudge each parameter to reduce the model's error. It only happens during training.
Running data through the model from input to output once, producing a prediction. Inference is forward-only; training also runs a backward pass.
Parameters that are held fixed and not updated. During inference they are frozen, which is why your chat can't permanently teach the model anything.
A short, cheaper extra round of training on your own examples to change how a model behaves, without building it from scratch.
The model's short-term working memory within one conversation. It lets a model recall earlier messages during inference, but it is not learning and it resets each new chat.
